Final Exam
• Date and time: Tuesday 9th December 2024, 9:45 – 12:00 pm (AEST)
• Duration: 2-hour writing time + 15 minutes reading time
• Venue: UNSW Campus
• Format: Multiple-choice questions (80%) and coding (20%) using Inspera Safe
 Mode
• Content: Lecture, lab, individual assignment, group assignment, and quizzes,
 except the Interactive Widgets in Week 7, File Operations about JSON files in
 Week 8.

• More details in Week 10 Lecture!

Outline
• Introduction to Machine Learning

• Regression

• Classification
                                                                                                                          9
    Introduction
    • Machine learning is making great strides
           ⎻ Large, good data sets
           ⎻ Computation power
           ⎻ Advances in algorithms
    • Many interesting applications
           ⎻ Computer vision: handwritten digits to self
             driving cars
           ⎻ Cybersecurity: spam detection
           ⎻ Finance: fraud detection, automate trading
             activities
           ⎻ Healthcare: improve efficiency and accuracy
             of diagnostic process
           ⎻ Business: predict customer behaviors

Source: https://www.forbes.com/sites/kalevleetaru/2019/01/15/why-machine-learning-needs-semantics-not-just-statistics/       9
                                                                    10
What is Learning?
• Learning denotes changes in the system that are adaptive in
 the sense that they enable the system to do the same task or
 tasks drawn from the same population more effectively the next
 time. – Simon, 1983


• Learning is making useful changes in our minds. – Minsky, 1985

• Learning is improving automatically with experience. –
 Mitchell, 1997
                                                                                                             11
    Machine Learning
    • Artificial Intelligence can be Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) or Artificial Narrow
       Intelligence (ANI)
         ⎻ ANI (or weak AI) is limited to one or a few specific tasks and can do it better than
            humans, such as classifying data or estimating a numeric variable
         ⎻ AGI (or strong AI) can learn to perform any task humans do, and allows machine to
            have full human cognitive abilities

                                                                               • Machine Learning is the
                                                                                field of study that gives
                                                                                computers the ability to
                                                                                learn without being
                                                                                explicitly programmed. As
                                                                                of now, ML is achieving
                                                                                ANI, and we are far from
                                                                                achieving AGI

Source: https://www.edureka.co/blog/ai-vs-machine-learning-vs-deep-learning/
                                                                              12
The History
• 1946: First computer called ENIAC to perform numerical computations.
• 1950: Alan Turing proposes the Turing test.
• 1952: First game playing program for checkers by Arthur Samuel at IBM.
 Knowledge based systems such as ELIZA and MYCIN.
• 1957: Perceptron developed by Frank Roseblatt. Can be combined to
 form a neural network.
• Early 1990’s: Statistical learning theory. Emphasize learning from data
 instead of rule-based inference.
• Current status: Machine learning used widely in industry, combination of
 various approaches but data-driven is prevalent.
                                                                                 13
Machine Learning Tasks
• Supervised Learning
   ⎻ Regression: predict numerical values
   ⎻ Classification: predict categorical values, i.e., labels
• Unsupervised Learning
   ⎻ Clustering: group data according to "distance"
   ⎻ Association: find frequent co-occurrences
   ⎻ Data reduction: project features to fewer features
• Reinforcement Learning – A machine learning approach where an agent learns
 to make optimal decisions by interacting with an environment and receiving
 feedback in the form of rewards or punishments
   ⎻ AlphaGo improved and became increasingly stronger and better at learning
     and decision-making
                                                                                                                   14
          Supervised Learning
          We use the independent variables (a.k.a. predictors 𝑥1 , … , 𝑥𝑛 ) to predict the
          dependent variable(s) (a.k.a. target or response 𝑦), through training a model
          using labelled data (𝒚|𝒙𝟏 , … , 𝒙𝒏 ).



Labelled data                                                                     Machine Learning
                                   Training          Labelled Data                   Algorithm
            𝑑𝑜𝑔,1        𝑑𝑜𝑔,1
(y1 = 𝑑𝑜𝑔|𝑥1      , … , 𝑥𝑛     )
  2         𝑑𝑜𝑔,2        𝑑𝑜𝑔,2
(y = 𝑑𝑜𝑔|𝑥1       , … , 𝑥𝑛     )
  3        𝑐𝑎𝑡,3        𝑐𝑎𝑡,3
(y = 𝑐𝑎𝑡|𝑥1 , … , 𝑥𝑛 )
             ⋮
                                                                                    Learned          Prediction
                                   Prediction          New Data                      model



                                      New data
                                       𝑥11 , … , 𝑥𝑛1 → 𝑦1 = Dog? Cat? Chicken?
                                       𝑥12 , … , 𝑥𝑛2 → 𝑦 2 = Dog? Cat? Chicken?
                                                               ⋮
                                                                           15
Regression
• Investigating the relationship between independent variables and the
 dependent variable (continuous), and to predict the dependent variable
• Examples
   ⎻ Predicting the price of a house given house features
   ⎻ Predicting the impact of SAT/GRE scores on college admissions
   ⎻ Predicting the sales based on input parameters
                                                                            16
Classification
• Algorithms (e.g., decision tree) learn how to assign a class label to
 the dependent variable (categorical), based on independent
 variables
• Examples
   ⎻ Given an email’s various attributes, classify if it is spam or not.
   ⎻ Given a post on twitter, classify if it is fake information or not.




                                                                            16
                                                                           17
Binary vs Multi-class Classification
• A classification problem with only 2 classes is referred to as binary
 classification
  ⎻ The output labels are 0 or 1; True or False; a or b, etc.
  ⎻ E.g., fake news or not; spam or no-spam email; buy or sell
• A problem with 3 or more classes is referred to as multi-class
 classification
                                                                18
Exercise 1
In order to understand what factors affect CEO salary, we
collect data about the top 500 firms in the US and develop a
model of CEO salary as a function of industry, number of
employees, and profit.
• What is the dependent variable?
• What are the independent variable(s)?
• Is the model a regressor or a classifier?
                                                                  19
Exercise 1 Solution
In order to understand what factors affect CEO salary, we
collect data about the top 500 firms in the US and develop a
model of CEO salary as a function of industry, number of
employees, and profit.
• Dependent variable: CEO Salary
• Independent variables: Industry, number of employees, profit
• The model is a regressor, since the dependent variable is
 continuous
                                                                 20
Exercise 2
We're considering launching a new product and wish to know
whether it will succeed or fail. We collect data about twenty
similar products that have already been launched and build a
model of whether they succeeded or failed, based on the
marketing budget, the price charged for the product, and the
competition price.
• What is the dependent variable?
• What are the independent variable(s)?
• Is the model a regressor or a classifier?
                                                                            21
Exercise 2 Solution
We're considering launching a new product and wish to know whether
it will succeed or fail. We collect data about twenty similar products
that have already been launched and build a model of whether they
succeeded or failed, based on the marketing budget, the price charged
for the product, and the competition price.
• Dependent variable: Whether the product will succeed or fail
• Independent variables: Marketing budget, price charged, competition
 price
• The model is a classifier, since the dependent variable is categorical
                                                                        22
Exercise 3
In order to predict the percentage change in the USD-Euro exchange
rate in the world stock markets each week we collect weekly data for
all of 2012 and build a model of the percentage change in the USD-
Euro exchange rate in terms of the percentage change in the US
market, the percentage change in the British market, and the
percentage change in the German market.
• What is the dependent variable?
• What are the independent variable(s)?
• Is the model a regressor or a classifier?
                                                                             23
Exercise 3 Solution
In order to predict the percentage change in the USD-Euro exchange rate
in the world stock markets each week we collect weekly data for all of
2012 and build a model of the percentage change in the USD-Euro
exchange rate in terms of the percentage change in the US market, the
percentage change in the British market, and the percentage change in the
German market.
• Dependent variable: Percentage change in USD-Euro exchange rate
• Independent variables: Percentage change in the US market, percentage
 change in the British market, percentage change in the German market
• The model is a regressor, since the dependent variable is continuous

                                                                                              28
    Linear Regression Example (1/2)
    • kc_house_data.csv data set contains information about 21,613 houses that were sold
       between May 2014 and May 2015 in King County
    • Variables, i.e., columns:
          ⎻ price: Price in dollars
          ⎻ sqft_living: Square footage of the entire house
          ⎻…
    • We aim to use this data to predict the price of a house, based on factors that could
       impact the price
    • Let’s load the data set and generate a new column ‘price_inMillion’
        import pandas as pd
        df = pd.read_csv("kc_house_data.csv")
        df['price_inMillion'] = df["price"]/1000000
        display(df)
Source: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/harlfoxem/housesalesprediction
 Linear Regression Example (2/2)
The scatterplot shows the relationship between price (in million dollars) and
square footage of the entire house (in square feet) in the entire data set.
Let’s use only square footage to predict price.



                                            Dependent variable?
                                            Independent variable(s)?
                                            How do we generate this scatter
                                            plot with a linear fit, step by step?
 Scatter Plot: Price vs. Square                                    30

Footage of the House
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.scatter(df.sqft_living, df.price_inMillion, color='Gray')
plt.xlabel("Square footage of the house", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Price (in Million)", fontsize=12)
plt.title('Price vs. Square footage of the house', fontsize=14)
 Introduction to Simple Linear                                                     31

Regression (SLR)
• Only one independent variable is used in SLR
• Intercept
   ⎻ When 𝑥 = 0, 𝑦 is expected to equal the intercept
   ⎻ Maybe meaningless in context of the data, and only serve to adjust
     the height of the line
• Slope: the change of 𝑦 for a one unit change in 𝑥
          𝑦 = 𝑏0 + 𝑏1 ∗ 𝑥 + 𝜀                         𝑦                   slope
             Intercept Slope     Error term

                                                          intercept
                                                                      𝑥
                                                                               32
Calculate Slope and Intercept in SLR
Slope and Intercept in SLR are the parameters of the fitted line and can be
calculated by sklearn.linear_model.LinearRegression()
       from sklearn import linear_model
       regr = linear_model.LinearRegression()
       price = df[['price_inMillion']]
       square_footage = df[['sqft_living']]
       regr.fit(square_footage, price) #fit() expects 2D data for x and y
       print ('Slope (Beta Coefficients): ', regr.coef_[0][0])
       print ('Intercept: ',regr.intercept_[0])



Final model: PriceinMillion (estimated) = 0.00028 * sqft_living – 0.04358
                                33
A Good Model or A Bad Model?
                                                                             34
Plotting the Regression Line
     predictPrice = regr.coef_[0][0]*square_footage + regr.intercept_[0]
     plt.plot(square_footage, predictPrice, '-r')
     plt.xlabel("Square footage of the house")
     plt.ylabel("Price (in Million)")
     plt.title('Price vs. Square footage of the house', fontsize = 14)




Final model: PriceinMillion (estimated) = 0.00028 * sqft_living – 0.04358
 Predicting Unknown Data using the                                                              35

Trained SLR
# Provide a list of square footages of new houses that have not been used to train the model
x = pd.DataFrame({'sqft_living':
                 [10300, 11400, 12500, 13550, 14600, 15650]})
y_pred = regr.predict(x) # we have created the model called regr
print('Predicted price (in Million):', y_pred, sep = '\n')
plt.scatter(x, y_pred, color = 'blue')
plt.xlabel("Square footage of the house")
plt.ylabel("Price (in Million)")
plt.title('Price vs. Square footage of the house', fontsize = 14)
plt.show()
                                                                                36
Combining All Charts Into One
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.scatter(df.sqft_living, df.price_inMillion, color='Gray')
from sklearn import linear_model
regr = linear_model.LinearRegression()
price = df[['price_inMillion']]
square_footage = df[['sqft_living']]
regr.fit(square_footage, price) #fit() expects 2D data for x and y
print ('Slope (Beta Coefficients): ', regr.coef_[0][0])
print ('Intercept: ',regr.intercept_[0])
predictPrice = regr.coef_[0][0]*square_footage + regr.intercept_[0]
plt.plot(square_footage, predictPrice, '-r')
plt.title('Price vs. Square footage of the house', fontsize = 14)
# Provide a list of square footages of new houses that have not been used to
train the model
x = pd.DataFrame({'sqft_living':
                 [10300, 11400, 12500, 13550, 14600, 15650]})
y_pred = regr.predict(x) # we have created the model called regr
print('Predicted price (in Million):', y_pred, sep = '\n')
plt.scatter(x, y_pred, color = 'blue')
plt.xlabel("Square footage of the house")
plt.ylabel("Price (in Million)")
plt.title('Price vs. Square footage of the house', fontsize = 14)
plt.show()
 Alternative Way using Seaborn                                     37

regplot() without the Prediction
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
sns.regplot(data=df, x="sqft_living", y="price_inMillion",
            scatter_kws={"color": "lightblue"},
            line_kws={"color": "red"})
plt.xlabel("Square footage of the house", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Price (in Million)", fontsize=12)
plt.title('Price vs. Square footage of the house', fontsize=14)

Outline
• Introduction to Machine Learning

• Regression

                                                                                                                                                     39
Workflow for Predictive Modeling
                                                                                                            • Step 1: Split labelled dataset into
                  1                                                                                           training and test sets
                                                                                                            • Step 2: Select a predictive model
                                                                                                              and feed training data into it to
                                                                                                              train the model
                                                                              3
                                                                                                            • Step 3: Feed test data into the
               2                                                                                              trained classifier model to predict
                                                                                                              the independent variable
                                                                      4                                     • Step 4: Evaluate the model
                                                                                                              performance using test data’s
                                                                                                              prediction accuracy

Source: https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Training-and-testing-our-machine-learning-approach_fig2_318132501
                                                                                                            40
1 Train-Test Split
 We split the data set into two sets
 • The training set (in-sample data or training data) is the set of data we analyze (train on) to design
   the rules in the model
 • The test set is a set of data we did NOT use to train our model, but use in the validation set to
   inform our choice of parameters/input features




  Source: https://builtin.com/data-science/train-test-split
   Let’s Use Salary Dataset to Illustrate                              41
1
  Train-test Split
 Variables in the salary dataset:
 • Purchased: binary variable (1 indicates purchase; 0 indicates no
  purchase)
 • UserID
 • Gender
 • Age
 • EstimatedSalary
 • 400 observations
                                                                               42
1 Independent vs. Dependent Variables
 • We aim to predict purchase behaviors by Age and EstimatedSalary
 • Independent variable: Age, EstimatedSalary
 • Dependent variable: Purchased (0 or 1)
 import pandas as pd
 datasets = pd.read_csv('Social_Network_Ads.csv')
 X = datasets.iloc[:, [2,3]].values   #Age and EstimatedSalary in 2D format
 Y = datasets.iloc[:, 4].values   #Purchased
                                                                                                                        43
1 Implement Train-test Split
  Our training vs. test datasets follow 80%:20% split.
  train_test_split() randomly splits the given dataset into
  train and test subsets


   from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

   X_Train, X_Test, Y_Train, Y_Test = train_test_split(X, Y, \
   test_size = 0.2, random_state = 0) #shuffle = True by default

   print(X_Train.shape)                                        #2D format; training set size
   print(X_Test.shape)                                                                                           20%
                                                                                                           80%



 Source: https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.model_selection.train_test_split.html
                                                                                                         44
Diversion: Feature Scaling
• This data processing step scales variable to have similar range
• It addresses the scenario that one variable with a large range (e.g., EstimatedSalary) outweighs
  other variables with a small range (e.g., Age) in terms of impact on the results

 Normalisation transforms the original data                Standardisation makes the transformed
 range into a newly specified data range, such             variable have mean of 0 and variance of 1,
 as [0,1], with preset target min and max                  i.e., standard normal distribution
              𝑥𝑜𝑟𝑖𝑔

 𝑚𝑖𝑛𝑜𝑟𝑖𝑔                                  𝑚𝑎𝑥𝑜𝑟𝑖𝑔


                 𝑥𝑛𝑒𝑤 = ?

            𝑚𝑖𝑛𝑛𝑒𝑤             𝑚𝑎𝑥𝑛𝑒𝑤

           𝑥𝑜𝑟𝑖𝑔 − 𝑚𝑖𝑛𝑜𝑟𝑖𝑔
 𝑥𝑛𝑒𝑤 =                     𝑚𝑎𝑥𝑛𝑒𝑤 − 𝑚𝑖𝑛𝑛𝑒𝑤 + 𝑚𝑖𝑛𝑛𝑒𝑤
          𝑚𝑎𝑥𝑜𝑟𝑖𝑔 − 𝑚𝑖𝑛𝑜𝑟𝑖𝑔
 Diversion: We Implement                                               45

Standardisation for This dataset
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
sc_X = StandardScaler()
X_Train = sc_X.fit_transform(X_Train) #learn the scaling parameters

X_Test = sc_X.transform(X_Test)
#use the same mean and variance to transform the test data




           X_Train before                    X_Train after
                                     46
2 Select a Model

 Common models for classification
 • Logistic Regression
 • Decision Tree
 • Support Vector Machine
 • Artificial Neural Network
 (More in INFS5720)
                                                                               47
2 Introduction of Logistic Regression
 • To predict the probability of a person purchasing or not
 • Use a sigmoid function 𝜎 𝑧 = 1+𝑒1 −𝑧 that maps the linear combination of
  independent variables to a value between 0 and 1
 • If the predicted probability is larger than threshold (default 0.5), the
  prediction is 1 (the person will purchase)
 • Otherwise, the prediction is 0 (no purchase)
                                                                                48
2 Implement Logistic Regression
 from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression



 classifier = LogisticRegression(solver = 'liblinear', random_state = 0)
 #initialize a model object



                                       Create a new LogisticRegression Model
 classifier.fit(X_Train, Y_Train)
   Feed Test Data into the Trained                                     49
3
  Classifier Model
 • We now predict the probability of purchase behaviors in the test
   data
 • Label ‘Purchased’ column based on the model’s prediction
 Y_Probs = classifier.predict_proba(X_Test)
 #X_test should be in a 2-D format

 Y_Pred = classifier.predict(X_Test)
   Evaluate the Model Performance                                     50
4
  using Test Data’s Prediction Accuracy
 • A classifier assigns an observation (a row) in the test data to
  one of a predefined set of classes, e.g., binary outcome 0 or 1
 • Then we use evaluation metrics to understand a machine
  learning model's performance, as well as its strengths and
  weaknesses
 • A common structure is Confusion Matrix, from which we can
  obtain Accuracy, True Positive Rate, True Negative Rate, etc.
   Confusion Matrix and Metrics for a                                                                        51
4
  Binary Classifier
                                                         Accuracy

                                                         (TP + TN) / (TP + FN + FP + TN)

                                                         True positive rate (TPR), or sensitivity, Recall

                                                         TP / (TP + FN)
                                                         Out of all the events, what percentage are
                                                         predicted correctly as positive?
   Confusion matrix for a binary classifier
from sklearn import metrics
cm = metrics.confusion_matrix(Y_Test, Y_Pred)            True negative rate (TNR), or Specificity
print(cm)
TP = cm[0, 0]                                            TN / (TN + FP)
FP = cm[1, 0]
FN = cm[0, 1]                                            Out of all the non-events, what percentage
TN = cm[1, 1]                                            are predicted correctly as negative?
Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confusion_matrix
             52
Questions
